questions.question: Which keyword is used to declare a function that can access the private members of a class without being a member of that class?
questions.explanation: The 'friend' keyword declares a non-member function (or another class) that is granted access to the private and protected members of the class.

questions.question: What does the 'this' pointer refer to inside a member function?
choices: The object that called the member function
The first parameter of the function
The base class of the object
questions.explanation: The 'this' pointer is an implicit pointer available in every non-static member function. It points to the object that invoked the function, allowing the object to refer to itself.

questions.question: What is a static member variable in a class?
choices: A variable that cannot be changed after initialization
A variable that is shared by all objects of the class
A variable that is only accessible inside constructors
A variable that is allocated on the stack
questions.explanation: A static member variable belongs to the class itself rather than to any individual object. All objects of the class share the same copy of the static variable.

questions.question: When is a copy constructor called?
choices: When an object is assigned to another existing object using =
When an object is initialized with another object of the same class
When a static member is accessed
questions.explanation: A copy constructor is called when a new object is being created as a copy of an existing object — during initialization, pass by value, or return by value. Assignment to an existing object uses the assignment operator instead.

questions.question: To overload the + operator for a class, you define a member function named operator___.
questions.explanation: Operator overloading uses the keyword 'operator' followed by the operator symbol. For example, 'MyClass operator+(const MyClass &right)' overloads the + operator.

questions.question: A ___ member variable is shared by all objects of a class rather than each object having its own copy.
questions.explanation: Static member variables exist as a single copy shared among all instances of the class. They must be defined outside the class declaration and exist even if no objects have been created.
